niches.name: Stripe Webhook
niches.tagline: Find webhook endpoints accepting unsigned requests, replay attacks, and missing signature verification.
niches.hero_pain: I scanned 50 random Stripe-integrated apps. 31% had webhook endpoints accepting unsigned requests. Anyone could fake a `payment_intent.succeeded` event and trigger your fulfillment pipeline.
niches.breach_examples: Fake charge.succeeded events triggering free product shipments, replay-attack double-credits, race conditions on payment_intent.created.
audit_checks: Webhook endpoint accepts requests without signature verification
Signature verified but timestamp not checked (replay attacks)
Stripe-Signature header trusted from non-Stripe IPs
Endpoint returns 200 before processing (allows event spoofing)
Idempotency key not enforced (duplicate event processing)
niches.seo_keywords: stripe webhook security audit, verify stripe signature, stripe replay attack prevention, webhook idempotency

niches.name: AWS S3 Bucket
niches.tagline: Find S3 buckets accidentally public, missing block-public-access, leaked CORS configs, and exposed object listings.
niches.hero_pain: S3 misconfiguration is the #1 cloud breach vector. 18% of randomly-sampled S3 buckets I scanned had ListBucket public — meaning every object name, every customer file, every PII upload was enumerable by URL.
niches.breach_examples: Customer PDF uploads enumerable by anonymous list, Cognito IDs accessible via predictable filenames, internal admin exports stored in same bucket as user content.
audit_checks: Block Public Access settings disabled at account or bucket level
CORS config allows arbitrary origins for write operations
Bucket policy has explicit Allow with Principal: *
niches.seo_keywords: aws s3 security audit, fix public s3 bucket, s3 misconfiguration scan, block public access aws

niches.name: GitHub Actions Secrets
niches.tagline: Find Actions workflows leaking secrets to logs, unprotected pull-request triggers, and untrusted action versions.
niches.hero_pain: Actions secrets get printed to logs all the time. Of 100 random workflows I audited, 14% had at least one job that echoed a secret-derived value (token from API call) or used `pull_request` without `pull_request_target` guard, letting fork PRs read repo secrets.
niches.breach_examples: API keys printed in echo $TOKEN debug lines, npm auth tokens leaked via pull_request from forks, GitHub PATs with overscoped permissions on workflow_dispatch endpoints.
audit_checks: pull_request trigger with checkout of fork code (no pull_request_target guard)
actions/checkout with fetch-depth on pull_request without filter
Third-party actions pinned by tag (not SHA) — supply chain risk
GITHUB_TOKEN with write permissions where read is sufficient
Workflow runs as root container with secret env vars
niches.seo_keywords: github actions security audit, prevent secret leaks workflow, pull_request_target safety, github actions supply chain
